name: Honda Tadakatsu
description: Honda Tadakatsu (1548 – 1610), also known as Honda Heihachirō, was a Japanese samurai general of the late Sengoku through early Edo periods, who served Tokugawa Ieyasu. He is renowned as one of the Four Heavenly Kings of the Tokugawa, and considered by many to be the greatest samurai warrior of all time, never suffering a significant wound in any of the many battles he participated in throughout his career. Tadakatsu was known for his immense strength, his skill with the spear, and his unwavering loyalty to the Tokugawa clan. His most famous weapon was his spear, Tonbokiri (Dragonfly Cutter), so named because the blade was so sharp that a dragonfly that landed on it would be instantly cut in two. He also wore a distinctive helmet adorned with deer antlers, making him easily recognizable on the battlefield. Tadakatsu fought in many pivotal battles, including the Battle of Anegawa, the Battle of Mikatagahara, the Battle of Nagashino, and the Battle of Sekigahara, playing a crucial role in Tokugawa Ieyasu's rise to power and the establishment of the Tokugawa shogunate. He retired from military service after the Battle of Sekigahara and dedicated his later years to administrative duties and training future generations of samurai. His legendary status and unparalleled battlefield record have cemented his place as a symbol of samurai valor and unwavering dedication in Japanese history and folklore.
subtitle: The Unvanquished Warrior of the Tokugawa
achievements: One of the Four Heavenly Kings of the Tokugawa
Symbol of samurai loyalty and valor

keyMyths.title: The Unscathed Warrior
keyMyths.description: Legend states that Honda Tadakatsu participated in over 57 major battles and numerous skirmishes, yet he never received a significant wound, a testament to his skill and luck. This invincibility became a cornerstone of his legendary status.

keyMyths.title: The Dragonfly Cutter
keyMyths.description: Tadakatsu's spear, Tonbokiri, was renowned for its sharpness. The name itself implies that even a dragonfly landing on its blade would be sliced in two, highlighting the weapon's incredible cutting power.
keyMyths.source: Legends and historical records related to weaponry

keyMyths.title: Loyalty to Tokugawa
keyMyths.description: Honda Tadakatsu's unwavering loyalty to Tokugawa Ieyasu is a recurring theme in stories about him. He remained steadfast in his service, even during times of great peril and uncertainty, solidifying his reputation as the ideal samurai.
keyMyths.source: Biographies of Tokugawa Ieyasu and Honda Tadakatsu

sources.author: Stephen Turnbull
sources.work: Samurai: The World of the Warrior
sources.description: Provides historical context and information on samurai culture and warfare.
